content:
11 min read
Just now

--

The happy path proves that an interface can work. Failure reveals whether users can still understand, trust, and recover from it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I used to design frontend features around the successful result.
A page loaded data, rendered the interface, accepted an action, and displayed the updated state. Loading received a spinner. Errors received a toast. Once those pieces existed, the feature felt complete.
That model worked well during demos.
Real interfaces were less cooperative. Requests overlapped. Connections disappeared. Data became stale. One operation succeeded while another failed. A user clicked twice because the button still looked active. A timeout made it impossible to tell whether the server had completed the action.
The components could remain small. The code could remain readable. The design system could remain consistent.
The interface could still become difficult to trust.
What changed my thinking was realizing that failure is not a separate screen added after the main design. It is part of the state model that determines what the user can see, what the system actually knows, and what action remains safe.
The Happy Path Was Quietly Defining the Whole Interface
A typical data-driven component often begins with a structure like this:
function AccountPage() {
const { data, isLoading, error } = useAccount();
if (isLoading) return <Spinner />;
if (error) return <ErrorMessage />;
return <AccountDetails account={data} />;
}For a small page, this may be entirely reasonable.
The problem appears when loading, error, and success are treated as the only realities the interface can represent.
What happens when useful data already exists and a background refresh begins? What if account details load successfully but billing information does not? What if the API returns no account because the user lacks permission rather than because the service is unavailable?
The component has handled three code branches, but it has not necessarily modeled three meaningful user states.
A full-screen spinner assumes that existing content is no longer useful. A single error branch assumes every failure blocks the same part of the page. A successful data value assumes the screen is fully healthy.
Those assumptions often enter the interface through control flow before anyone discusses them as product decisions.
The conditionals become the design.
I now ask different questions before deciding what the loading and error branches should look like.
What can remain visible during refresh? Which actions depend on fresh data? Which failure blocks the page, and which failure belongs to one section? Can the user recover without losing work? Does the interface know that the operation failed, or does it only know that confirmation never arrived?
Those questions usually reveal more about the frontend architecture than the first component tree does.
Three Booleans Could Not Represent What the User Needed to Know
One of my biggest mistakes was modeling complex flows with a few independent flags:
type RequestState = {
isLoading: boolean;
hasError: boolean;
isSuccess: boolean;
};The model is convenient because it maps directly to conditional rendering.
It also allows combinations that are unclear or impossible.
Can isLoading and isSuccess both be true during a background refresh? If useful data exists while a secondary request has failed, is the page successful or failed? If a remote operation may have completed but the response was lost, should the interface show an error?
The problem is not that booleans are inherently bad. The problem is that they often erase distinctions the user needs.
For an account screen, the useful states may look more like this:
type AccountViewState =
| { status: "initial-loading" }
| { status: "ready"; account: Account }
| { status: "refreshing"; account: Account }
| {
status: "partial";
account: Account;
billingError: AppError;
}
| { status: "empty" }
| { status: "unauthorized" }
| { status: "unavailable"; error: AppError };This is more verbose than three flags.
It is also more honest.
The model makes it difficult to accidentally remove useful content during refresh. It separates an empty result from an inaccessible resource. It gives partial failure a place instead of forcing the entire page into one generic error branch.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Not every interaction needs a formal state machine or a large discriminated union. A small toggle or local menu can remain simple.
The extra structure becomes useful when different states require different decisions.
If one failure requires signing in, another requires correcting input, and a third requires waiting for confirmation, combining them into hasError removes information the interface needs.
The state model should be as detailed as the user’s next safe action.
Refreshing Should Not Erase Useful Information
I once treated every request as a reason to replace the current interface with a loading state.
The implementation was easy to understand. A request started, the content disappeared, and a spinner appeared until the new response arrived.
The result felt unstable.
A user could be reading account details when a background refresh blanked the page. Changing a filter could remove an entire table before the replacement data was ready. A temporary network failure could turn a useful dashboard into an error screen even though the previous information was still meaningful.
The request state and the display state were too tightly coupled.
Initial loading and refreshing are not the same experience.
Initial loading means the interface has no useful data yet. A skeleton, placeholder, or focused loading indicator may be appropriate.
Refreshing means the interface already has information and is checking for a newer version. Preserving the current content is often more useful than replacing it.
The UI may show a subtle refresh indicator, mark the data as stale, display the last successful update time, or disable only the actions that require freshness.
There are valid exceptions.
A trading screen, inventory reservation flow, or permission-sensitive action may require current data before allowing the next step. In those cases, stale information may still be visible for context while risky actions remain unavailable.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The important decision is whether stale information is merely imperfect or actively dangerous.
A frontend that hides all stale data creates unnecessary disruption. A frontend that presents stale data as current may enable incorrect decisions.
Failure-aware design communicates both usefulness and uncertainty.
It does not ask only:
Do we have data?
It asks:
What do we know, how current is it, and what is still safe to do?
Errors Belong Near the Decision They Affect
Global error toasts are convenient.
They require little layout work, avoid changing the component structure, and provide one consistent place for failure messages. They are useful when a failure affects the whole application or has no natural location.
I relied on them too heavily.
A toast saying “Something went wrong” often appears after the user’s attention has moved elsewhere. It does not explain which action failed, whether anything changed, or what should happen next.
Consider a profile page with separate controls for updating contact information, changing a password, and uploading an avatar.
If the avatar upload fails, replacing the whole page with an error state would be excessive. A generic toast is usually too vague.
The failure belongs near the upload control because that is where the user must decide what to do next. The selected image may need to remain visible. The retry action should preserve the file. The rest of the form should remain usable.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Error placement communicates failure scope.

• An inline message says this field or action failed.

• A section-level state says this part of the screen is unavailable.

• A page-level state says the primary purpose of the page cannot continue.
Some failures need visibility at more than one level. A local control may need inline guidance while the application also records an operational failure for monitoring or shows a broader session warning.
The message also needs to preserve the difference between failure categories.
“Enter a valid email address” requires correction.
“Your session expired” requires authentication.
“We could not confirm whether the update completed” requires verification before retrying.
“Service unavailable” may justify waiting.
When every problem becomes “Something went wrong,” the frontend has removed the information required for recovery.
A Submitted Action Was Not Yet a Confirmed Result
Data fetching made failure visible. Mutations changed how I thought about frontend responsibility.
A mutation is not only a request. It is a transition between what the user intended, what the interface currently shows, and what the server has confirmed.
Suppose a user changes an order from pending to approved.
The interface could wait for the server before changing anything. This is safer but may feel slow.
It could update immediately and roll back on failure. This feels responsive but requires a reliable previous state and a meaningful rollback.
It could display an intermediate state:
type ApprovalState =
| { status: "idle" }
| { status: "approving"; operationId: string }
| { status: "approved"; orderId: string }
| { status: "rejected"; reason: string }
| { status: "unknown"; operationId: string };The important distinction is between user intent and confirmed system state.
An optimistic update assumes success and changes the visible result immediately. A pending state acknowledges the action without claiming that the server has accepted it.
The right choice depends on the consequence of being wrong.
Optimistic updates work well for low-risk, reversible actions such as starring an item or changing a local preference.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

They deserve more caution when the operation changes permissions, moves money, reserves limited inventory, or triggers an irreversible external effect.
For higher-risk mutations, I prefer visible intermediate states and explicit confirmation. The button should indicate that work is in progress. Repeated clicks should not create duplicate requests. Related controls may need to remain locked while unrelated parts of the page stay usable.
Most importantly, the interface should not display certainty the system does not possess.
Retry Is a Workflow, Not Just a Button
Adding a “Try again” button can make an error state look complete.
The harder question is what retrying actually does.
For a read request, retrying is usually straightforward. The client sends another request and replaces the result.
For a mutation, retrying may repeat a side effect.
A payment, invitation, account creation, or inventory reservation may have succeeded remotely even though the browser received a timeout. Sending the same request again can create duplicates unless the operation was designed to be repeatable.
The frontend cannot solve this alone, but it participates in the contract.
A retry-safe mutation may require:

• a stable operation identifier;

• server-side idempotency;

• a visible pending or unknown state;

• a status endpoint for reconciliation;

• protection against repeated clicks;

• language that does not claim definite failure too early.
This changes the interface.
Instead of saying:
Payment failed. Try again.
The UI may need to say:
Confirmation was not received. We are checking the transaction status.
That message is less simple, but it is more accurate.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Retry behavior also needs limits. Infinite automatic retries can waste battery, overload a struggling service, and keep the interface in a misleading pending state. Manual
